mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let B \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the dependent sum projection \pi _1 \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx \right ) \mathclose {} \to A creates a path object structure \mathopen {} \left \{ x:A \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} on \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx as follows:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx \\ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } x \end {aligned}

tree.mainmatter: Univalence follows from univalence of \mathcal {A} and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

frontmatter.title: Path objects and the univalence axiom

mainmatter.p: A reflexive graph \mathcal {A} \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} , \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } , \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a type \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} of vertices together with a family of types \mathopen {} \left ( \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Type} of edges and an assignment \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } x of reflexivity data to each vertex. The reflexive graph is called univalent when for all x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y is a proposition, and so is contractible with centre \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} \right ) \mathclose {}.
We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.

The intrinsic path object on a type A, written \mathord { \vartriangle } A, is defined by setting x \approx _{ \mathord { \vartriangle } A } y : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( x = y \right ) \mathclose {} and \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathord { \vartriangle } A } {x} : \equiv \mathsf {refl}.
That the reflexive graph defined above is univalent follows immediately by identification induction.
The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

mainmatter.p: The reflexive graph structure on \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} by homotopy and identity homotopies is univalent.

Given a dependent function f : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, if each component Bx is contractible with centre fx then the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} is contractible with centre f.
This follows immediately from univalence for functions.
The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.

Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of propositions; fixing f \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, it suffices to show any other g \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx may be identified with f. As B is a family of propositions, we know that each Bx is contractible with centre fx. Therefore, the closure of contractible types under dependent products () shows that \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx is contractible with centre f.

Let \mathcal {B} \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a family of path objects. We define the product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathcal {B} x of the family \mathcal {B} to be the following path object:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {} \\ f \approx _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathcal {B} x } g &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } f x \approx _{ \mathcal {B} x } g x \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathcal {B} x } {f} &: \equiv \lambda x \mathpunct {.} \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} x } \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}

mainmatter.p: Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products () via the distributivity of \prod over \sum.
Let \mathcal {A} , \mathcal {B} \colon \mathsf {PathObj} be two path objects; the binary product of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} is the path object \mathcal {A} \times \mathcal {B} specified below:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \times \mathcal {B} \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \times \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \\ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathcal {A} \times \mathcal {B} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,u \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \times u \approx _{ \mathcal {B} } v \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} \times \mathcal {B} } \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x}, \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} } {u} \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}

mainmatter.p: The univalence property of \mathcal {A} \times \mathcal {B} follows immediately from that of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B}.

Let \mathcal {A} be a path object and let B be a type. A function f \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to B that admits a quasi-inverse g \colon B \to A induces a path object structure on B by pushforward:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert f_* \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv B \\ x \approx _{ f_* \mathcal {A} } y &: \equiv gx \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } gy \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ f_* \mathcal {A} } x &: \equiv \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } \mathopen {} \left ( gx \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}

mainmatter.p: We first prove the lemma for biinvertible functions, so that both the section and retraction are tracked by g. In this case, we may use singleton induction via the univalence principle for types to restrict attention to the case that f is the identity map, and the result is immediate.

Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let B \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the dependent sum projection \pi _1 \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx \right ) \mathclose {} \to A creates a path object structure \mathopen {} \left \{ x:A \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} on \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx as follows:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx \\ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } x \end {aligned}

tree.mainmatter: Univalence follows from univalence of \mathcal {A} and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

mainmatter.p: A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.

A reflexive graph \mathcal {A} \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} , \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } , \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a type \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} of vertices together with a family of types \mathopen {} \left ( \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Type} of edges and an assignment \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } x of reflexivity data to each vertex. The reflexive graph is called univalent when for all x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y is a proposition, and so is contractible with centre \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} \right ) \mathclose {}.
We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.
The correspondence between biinvertible and bijective maps of and lifts to an equivalence from the type of equivalences to the type of bijections.
By and via .
